questions.question: The 2025 APA meta-analysis found what type of relationship between screen time and child mental health?

options.text: Bidirectional: increased screens lead to problems AND children with problems use screens more, creating reinforcing cycle
Only mental health causes screen use

questions.explanation: The relationship is bidirectional: increased screen time leads to emotional/behavioral problems, AND children with socioemotional problems are more likely to use screens to cope, creating a reinforcing cycle. Breaking this requires addressing BOTH screen limits AND underlying mental health needs. Limits alone fail if emotional needs aren't met.

questions.question: According to updated AAP guidelines, what matters MORE than total screen time hours?

options.text: How, when, what, and with whom screens are used - content quality and context shape impact more than time alone

questions.explanation: The AAP updated guidelines (2025-2026) moved from firm time limits toward quality-of-content and context approach. How screens are used (active vs. passive), when (not before bed), what content (educational vs. violent), and with whom (co-viewing vs. alone) shape impact more than total time. This acknowledges that video chatting grandparents differs dramatically from doomscrolling social media.

questions.question: For infants and toddlers (0-18 months), what is the AAP recommendation?

options.text: Avoid screens except video-chatting with family

questions.explanation: AAP recommends avoiding screens except video-chatting (FaceTime with relatives) for 0-18 months. Immature cognitive processing can't make sense of 2D screens. This is critical period for real-world sensory exploration, language development, and attachment formation. Screens displace crucial parent-child interaction. Video calls support family relationships and language exposure—that's the exception.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for screen time research?

options.text: Strong for association with problems; Moderate-Emerging for specific parenting guidance

questions.explanation: Evidence grade is STRONG for association between excessive screen time and problems (117 studies, n>292,000 children in 2025 meta-analysis). Evidence is MODERATE-EMERGING for specific parenting guidance—we know screens affect mental health but research on optimal management strategies is still developing.

questions.question: When does screen use require professional intervention?

options.text: Inability to reduce despite wanting to, lying/hiding use, significant functional impairment, withdrawal symptoms when removed, using to escape rather than address problems
Never - parents can always handle it

questions.explanation: Professional help needed for: inability to reduce use despite wanting to, lying about or hiding use, significant impairment in school/friendships/family, withdrawal symptoms (irritability, anxiety, aggression) when screens removed, using screens to escape problems rather than address them, sleep deprivation from nighttime use, or meeting Gaming Disorder criteria (impaired control, prioritizing gaming, continuation despite consequences for 12+ months).

title: Screen Time, Technology, and Digital Wellness - Quiz
